The same thing applies to this social media stuff. Dealing with all the fake content out there feels like trying to find a good connection in a thunderstorm—it’s messy, and sometimes you just want to shut the whole thing off. Since I don't spend hours glued to my phone, I’m not completely overwhelmed, but when I do check in, it’s important to keep my head straight. For me, the first step isn't about fighting every piece of garbage; it’s about building a firewall for my own peace of mind. I try to treat it like I’m checking a dodgy electrical panel—you gotta check the source before you assume the whole thing is fine. If something looks too crazy to be true, especially if it’s making you really agitated, I treat it with serious skepticism. I don't just scroll past it hoping it goes away; I stop and ask myself who is saying this and why. When I see something that feels manipulative, I usually just hit the report button. Instead of getting pulled into the drama, I mute or unfollow the source. It’s about protecting my own energy, much like making sure my own wiring is safe so I can keep working without a short circuit. It's about being discerning, not paranoid. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, when you’re out there working, whether it’s pulling wire or getting my hands dirty, you learn pretty quick that not everything is what it seems. The same thing applies to this social media stuff. Dealing with all the fake content out there feels like trying to find a good connection in a thunderstorm—it’s messy, and sometimes you just want to shut the whole thing off. Since I don't spend hours glued to my phone, I’m not completely overwhelmed, but when I do check in, it’s important to keep my head straight. For me, the first step isn't about fighting every piece of garbage; it’s about building a firewall for my own peace of mind. I try to treat it like I’m checking a dodgy electrical panel—you gotta check the source before you assume the whole thing is fine. If something looks too crazy to be true, especially if it’s making you really agitated, I treat it with serious skepticism. I don't just scroll past it hoping it goes away; I stop and ask myself who is saying this and why. Is this person someone I actually trust, like a friend who knows my family, or is it some anonymous account trying to stir up trouble? When I see something that feels manipulative, I usually just hit the report button. I don't feel like I have the energy to get into some big online fight, that just drains you, and frankly, I’ve got enough going on with the kids and running my own business. Instead of getting pulled into the drama, I mute or unfollow the source. It’s about protecting my own energy, much like making sure my own wiring is safe so I can keep working without a short circuit. It's about being discerning, not paranoid. You filter out the noise so you can focus on the real things—like a good game of calcio or a quiet weekend in the garden.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a self-employed electrician in Philadelphia, married with two children, possessing moderate extraversion and agreeableness, but low conscientiousness and openness. The tone should be practical, grounded, slightly weary of online drama, and direct, reflecting a hands-on, no-nonsense approach. The discussion needs to focus on practical coping mechanisms rather than theoretical analysis. The advice should lean towards self-protection and energy management, mirroring the practical nature of her trade and her desire to avoid unnecessary conflict (low conscientiousness/agreeableness in conflict).
